articles.title: Martha Stewart's AI Startup Hint Wants to Fix Your Home First
articles.summary: Martha Stewart co-founded Hint, an AI home management platform that monitors your home and flags problems before they become expensive disasters. The startup launched with $10 million in seed funding led by Slow Ventures. It all started at an Easter brunch on Stewart's farm, which is a very Martha Stewart origin story.
articles.content:
Martha Stewart has built an entire career telling people how to run a beautiful home. Now she wants an AI to do the heavy lifting for you. Stewart's new startup, Hint, is a home management platform that she announced alongside news of $10 million in seed funding. The idea is simple but genuinely useful: stop waiting for things to break and start managing your home like a pro.

It all started at Easter brunch on Stewart's farm, where she met her neighbor Kyle Rush, an AI engineer, and realized he was describing something she had imagined for years — software that notices a leaky ceiling, an expiring insurance policy, or too-high utility bills before the homeowner does. That idea became Hint, co-founded by Stewart, home-services veteran Yih-Han Ma, and Rush as CTO.

Hint is billed as an "always-on, AI-native home management platform" that gives homeowners real-time monitoring and personalized advice. Users enter their home address so Hint can pull data on everything from soil quality to flood risk, then combine that with know-how drawn from contractors, designers, and Stewart herself. Stewart's fingerprints are all over Hint, from the name to the app's visual language and even the logo's shade of green, which was chosen to match the eggs from her farm. She has tested Hint's outputs on her own property and written guides the product pulls from directly.

A 2025 Harvard housing study reported that Americans spend more than $500 billion a year on residential renovations and repairs, while Angi's 2025 home spending survey found 62% of homeowners were more worried about affording maintenance than they were the year prior. Hint's argument is that AI can sidestep the cost trap that has squeezed concierge-style home services at scale. The one question mark worth watching: when Hint connects users with products or services, it may earn affiliate or transaction fees, a setup that has quietly compromised the independence of countless "unbiased" consumer platforms before it. Hint's stated principle is that its recommendations stay blind to commercial deals, which is a big promise to keep as the money rolls in.

Hint will be available on desktop and for download on iOS in summer 2026. Stewart told Fortune: "I've wanted to create something beyond education, something that could actually help proactively manage one's home the way that I do. Hint is." If she can pull it off at scale, this could be the most useful thing she has ever put her name on.
keyPoints: Martha Stewart co-founded Hint, an AI home management platform that proactively monitors homes and flags issues before they become costly
Hint raised $10 million in seed funding led by Slow Ventures, with backing from Montauk Capital, Tusk Venture Partners, and others
The idea was born at an Easter brunch on Stewart's farm when she met co-founder and CTO Kyle Rush, her neighbor and an AI engineer
Hint will launch on desktop and iOS in summer 2026 and is currently accepting waitlist signups
Americans spend over $500 billion a year on home repairs, and 62% of homeowners say maintenance costs are getting harder to afford

articles.title: Solar Impulse 2 Broke a Flight Record Then Crashed Into the Gulf
articles.summary: Solar Impulse 2, the iconic solar-powered plane that flew around the entire world in 2016, has been lost at sea after crashing into the Gulf of Mexico on May 4, 2026 during an autonomous test flight operated by Skydweller Aero. The aircraft had just completed a record-breaking 8-day, 14-minute solar drone flight before losing power and plunging into international waters off Mississippi. The crash destroyed one of aviation's most historically significant aircraft and ends any hope of it being displayed at the Swiss Museum of Transport as planned.
articles.content:
Solar Impulse 2 was supposed to be a legend preserved in a museum. Instead it is at the bottom of the Gulf of Mexico. The famous Swiss solar-powered aircraft, which made history in 2016 by becoming the first plane to fly around the world using only solar energy, crashed into the Gulf of Mexico on May 4, 2026. There were no injuries because no one was on board. But the loss of the aircraft itself is a major blow to the world of clean aviation.

The story of how Solar Impulse 2 ended up as a drone is a fascinating one. The plane was built by Swiss pilots Bertrand Piccard and André Borschberg, who flew it across 43,000 kilometers through four continents, two oceans and three seas without burning a single drop of fuel. In 2019, the Solar Impulse Foundation sold Solar Impulse 2 to Skydweller Aero, a Spanish-American company with a very different vision. Skydweller wanted to convert it into a long-endurance, uncrewed surveillance drone for military and commercial clients, capable of loitering in the air for weeks at a time carrying radar, optical sensors, and communications gear.

The aircraft had actually just set a record before it went down. Skydweller Aero confirmed that Solar Impulse 2 took off from Stennis International Airport in Mississippi on April 26 and flew autonomously for 8 days and 14 minutes before crashing into the Gulf of Mexico on May 4. The company described the flight as part of a US Navy exercise, saying it "validates the reality of perpetual, solar-powered flight in a military mission-relevant environment." Preliminary reports from the National Transportation Safety Board indicate the aircraft suffered a sudden loss of power mid-flight and plunged into international waters off Bay St. Louis without the ability to recover altitude.

Piccard and Borschberg, the two pilots who originally flew Solar Impulse 2 around the world, said they only learned about the crash through social media. They called it "the loss of an important technological flagship." The aircraft had been due to return to Switzerland after Skydweller's program concluded for permanent display at the Swiss Museum of Transport in Lucerne. That will not happen now. The NTSB is investigating the accident, and Skydweller Aero faces tough questions about the future of its program.
keyPoints: Solar Impulse 2 crashed into the Gulf of Mexico on May 4, 2026 after losing power during an autonomous test flight
The aircraft had just completed a record-breaking 8 days and 14 minutes of solar-powered autonomous flight
Skydweller Aero bought and converted the iconic plane into an uncrewed military surveillance drone in 2019
Original pilots Bertrand Piccard and André Borschberg say they only learned about the crash through social media
The NTSB is investigating and the aircraft's planned museum display in Switzerland will now never happen

articles.title: Amazon Workers Are Faking AI Tasks Because Nobody Told Them What To Do
articles.summary: Amazon employees say the company is pressuring them to use AI tools more in their day-to-day work, but without giving them clear guidance on what that actually means. The result is workers inventing pointless tasks just to show they are hitting AI usage targets. It is a corporate AI mandate running headfirst into the gap between a company demanding adoption and a workforce left without a roadmap.
articles.content:
Amazon wants its employees to use more AI. That much is clear. What is far less clear, according to the workers themselves, is what they are actually supposed to be using it for. Fast Company spoke with Amazon employees who described a growing pressure to show AI adoption in their workflows, with performance expectations tied to AI usage metrics.

The problem with measuring something vague is that people find ways to hit the number without delivering the result. That is exactly what is happening at Amazon right now. Workers are describing a dynamic where, because the directive is broad and the use cases are poorly defined, they end up using AI tools on tasks that did not need to be done at all. The AI gets used, the box gets checked, and nothing productive actually happens. It is performative adoption rather than real transformation.

This is a bigger story than it might first appear. Amazon has spent billions positioning itself as an AI-first company, from its investment in Anthropic to the AI features baked into Amazon Web Services. Pushing that message internally makes sense from a leadership perspective. But mandates without direction create a very human response: people optimize for the metric, not the mission. If your manager wants to see AI usage go up, you find a way to make AI usage go up, even if the underlying work is busywork.

What Amazon is running into is a version of a classic management trap. When a company tells employees "do more of this" without telling them "here is why and here is how," creativity goes in the wrong direction. The pressure to show AI adoption is real. The strategy to channel it usefully, at least based on what employees are describing, has not quite caught up. It raises a pointed question for every company rushing to report AI integration to shareholders: are your workers actually working smarter, or are they just performing it for an audience?

For culture watchers, this is the real frontier of the AI era. It is not the models, it is the messy human layer on top of them. Amazon will not be the last company to discover that mandating innovation from the top without building the scaffolding beneath it tends to produce theater rather than change.
keyPoints: Amazon is pressuring employees to demonstrate more AI usage in their daily work
Workers say the guidance on what to actually use AI for is vague and unclear
The result is employees inventing unnecessary tasks just to hit AI usage targets
This is happening inside one of the world's biggest AI investors, which has poured money into Anthropic and Amazon Web Services AI features
The story shows the gap between top-down AI mandates and practical, productive adoption

articles.title: Dunkin's 48oz Beverage Buckets Hit Nationwide on May 22
articles.summary: Dunkin' is bringing its viral 48oz Beverage Buckets to participating locations nationwide on May 22, kicking off Memorial Day weekend, months after they sold out during a limited test in Massachusetts and New Hampshire. The buckets can be filled with iced coffee or a Dunkin' Refresher, and will be available only while supplies last. During the February test, coffee buckets started at $8.99 and refresher buckets at $9.49.
articles.content:
Dunkin' has been teasing coffee fans all year. Back in February, the chain quietly tested its 48oz Beverage Buckets at just a handful of locations in Massachusetts and New Hampshire. The buckets, which are literally bucket shaped containers with a handle, lid, and oversized straw, sold out almost instantly. People who could not get to New England were furious. One person on Instagram declared they would drive from Staten Island to Massachusetts just to get one.

Now the wait is over for the rest of the country. Food blogger Markie Devo revealed on Instagram on May 12 that Dunkin' will drop the 48oz Beverage Buckets at participating locations nationwide on May 22, right as Memorial Day weekend kicks off. Customers can get the bucket filled with either iced coffee or a Dunkin' Refresher. The nationwide pricing has not been confirmed yet, but during the original test the coffee bucket started at $8.99 and the refresher version was $9.49.

The reaction online has been exactly what you would expect. Comments ranged from "HAVE BEEN WAITINGGGGG" to "Sign me up for an iced coffee bucket anxiety attack." The bucket is genuinely enormous. At 48 ounces, it is double the size of a Dunkin' extra large hot drink and 50 percent bigger than the chain's largest cold drink cup. To put it simply: this is a lot of caffeine in one vessel.

The nationwide rollout also fits into a bigger moment for Dunkin'. The chain has been leaning hard into the refresher category in 2026, dropping new summer flavors and even launching powdered drink mix versions of four popular options. Dunkin' also announced plans this week to return to Canada after years away, aiming to compete directly with Tim Hortons. The catch with the buckets is that once a store runs out, they are gone. There is no indication Dunkin' is making these a permanent menu item, so May 22 is your best shot.
keyPoints: Dunkin' 48oz Beverage Buckets drop at participating locations nationwide on May 22
Buckets can be filled with iced coffee or a Dunkin' Refresher
During the February test, coffee buckets started at $8.99 and refreshers at $9.49
The original test in Massachusetts and New Hampshire sold out almost immediately
Supplies are limited and there is no confirmed plan to make the buckets a permanent menu item

articles.title: Wedding Content Is Now a Brand Deal. Meet the Monetised Bride.
articles.summary: Weddings have quietly transformed into full content productions, and brands, stylists, and publicists are now building businesses around viral bridal moments. What was once a personal milestone is increasingly a marketing opportunity, with couples and their big days becoming launching pads for sponsorships and social media campaigns. The result is a whole new economy sitting at the intersection of love, content, and commerce.
articles.content:
Something has shifted in the wedding industry, and it is not subtle. The moment couples start planning their big day, a new set of players shows up alongside the florist and the caterer. Brands want placement. Stylists want credit. Publicists want coverage. The wedding is no longer just a celebration. It is a content drop.

The logic makes total sense when you think about it. A wedding carries built-in emotional weight, a captive audience, and a near-guaranteed viral moment if the dress, the setting, or the first dance lands right. That is exactly the kind of organic-feeling content that brands pay enormous sums to manufacture on any other day. So why not monetise the real thing?

Stylists and publicists who once worked exclusively in entertainment are now pitching their services to brides and grooms. They negotiate gifted wardrobes, sponsored honeymoons, and brand partnerships built around the wedding content cycle. From the engagement announcement to the morning-after recap post, every frame is a potential placement. The couple gets perks. The brand gets reach. The agency takes a cut.

The uncomfortable part is the question it raises about what weddings are actually for. When the photographer is also shooting content for a dress label, and the venue was chosen partly for its grid aesthetic, the line between a genuine celebration and a branded activation gets genuinely blurry. For some couples that trade-off is worth it. For others, the pressure to perform their own wedding for an audience is exactly the kind of thing they were hoping to escape. Either way, the monetised bride is here, and she is very online.
keyPoints: Weddings are now treated as content productions with real viral and commercial potential
Brands, stylists, and publicists are building services specifically around bridal content deals
Couples can receive gifted products, sponsored travel, and brand partnerships tied to their wedding
The trend blurs the line between a personal milestone and a branded marketing campaign
Not all couples welcome the pressure, raising questions about authenticity in modern wedding culture

articles.title: Instagram Launches Instants: A Snapchat Clone With a Meta Twist
articles.summary: Instagram launched Instants on May 13, 2026, a new disappearing-photo feature that lives inside the main Instagram app and also comes as a standalone app of the same name. Photos can only be taken live through the camera, cannot be edited, and vanish once viewed or after 24 hours. It borrows heavily from Snapchat and BeReal, putting Instagram in direct competition with both.
articles.content:
Instagram is trying to go back to basics. On May 13, Meta globally launched Instants, a new feature built around spontaneous, unedited photo sharing with close friends. Instagram announced that it is globally launching Instants, a new feature for sharing authentic, disappearing photos after testing it with select users. The feature lets users share disappearing photos with their close friends or mutual followers that can be viewed only once and remain available for 24 hours.

The mechanics are simple, and very deliberately so. With Instants, you capture a photo with Instagram's in-app camera and are not allowed to edit the image. The format does not allow uploads from your camera, and although you can add text to your Instants, you cannot modify them any further. Meta has also developed a standalone Instants companion app for quicker camera access. The standalone app is a direct competitor to Snapchat, the original ephemeral image social network.

The app borrows ideas from social platforms like Snapchat, Locket, and BeReal, as it focuses on authentic and ephemeral content. This is not Instagram's first attempt at this kind of thing. This launch is not Instagram's first attempt at ephemeral sharing. Previous efforts included the 2014 app Bolt and a BeReal-inspired feature called Candid Stories, released in 2019. So the question is whether Instants can stick where those earlier efforts did not.

Although Instagram began as a way for friends to share moments with each other, the platform has gradually become overrun with influencer content and ads. With Instants, Instagram looks to be leaning back into more casual, private interactions centered around photo sharing among circles of friends. That is a real shift in tone, even if the product itself feels familiar.

For parents of teens, Instagram has baked in some extra guardrails. Parental supervision for teen accounts on Instagram is automatically applied to Instants, including shared time limits, safety tools such as a block on screenshots and screen recordings, and restricted access by default between 10 PM and 7 AM. A parent of a teen with a supervised account will get a notification when the teen downloads the Instants app. However, Instagram may be a bit late to capitalize on the trend of low-pressure, authentic photo sharing, as BeReal is not as popular as it once was, and many users already use Instagram Stories for fast, informal updates and might not see the need for a separate app and feature to do so. Still, with Instagram's reach, Instants has a much bigger runway than either BeReal or Bolt ever had.
keyPoints: Instagram launched Instants globally on May 13, a disappearing-photo feature inside the main app and as a standalone app
Photos must be taken live through the camera and cannot be edited or uploaded from a camera roll
Instants disappear once viewed and cannot be seen after 24 hours
Screenshots and screen recordings of Instants are blocked
Instagram has tried similar features before with the 2014 app Bolt and a 2019 BeReal-inspired feature called Candid Stories

articles.title: Variety's Cannes Rooftop Party Shook as Wooden Structure Buckled
articles.summary: Variety's Welcome to Cannes party at the JW Marriott Cannes turned dramatic when a wooden structure on the rooftop began to buckle under the weight of guests, sending partygoers scrambling. Witnesses described hearing a loud crunch of metal before the terrace shook violently. The night recovered quickly, with guests moving to a safer part of the terrace to continue celebrating.
articles.content:
Things got a little too real at Variety's Welcome to Cannes party on Wednesday night. The annual bash, held on the rooftop of the JW Marriott Cannes, was mid-swing when guests suddenly heard something very alarming.

"There was a loud noise, like breaking metal, it's so specific. A crunch of metal," one witness told Page Six. Then the rooftop shook violently. Panic spread fast, and guests scrambled to move away from the affected area.

The cause turned out to be a wooden structure that had been erected on the roof for the event. It began to buckle under the weight of the crowd gathered on it, forcing everyone to relocate to a different part of the terrace. No injuries were reported, and the party carried on.

Variety Co-President and Publisher Dea Lawrence addressed the room afterward with a line that only works if everyone is already safe. "Welcome to the most talked-about party at Cannes," she said, adding that she appreciated "all the strong and the brave in show business who stuck it out and stayed." The crowd that stuck around was a serious one. Guests included actresses Diane Kruger and Jordana Brewster, Academy of Motion Picture Arts and Sciences President Lynette Howell Taylor, and Sony Pictures Classics chiefs Michael Barker and Tom Bernard. The party was also honouring Thierry Frémaux, the director of the Cannes Film Festival, who is celebrating his 25th year leading the event.
keyPoints: A wooden structure on the JW Marriott Cannes rooftop buckled under the weight of partygoers
Guests heard a loud crunch of metal and felt the rooftop shake violently
Everyone was moved to a safer part of the terrace and no injuries were reported
The party was Variety's annual Welcome to Cannes event honouring Thierry Frémaux
Notable guests included Diane Kruger, Jordana Brewster, and AMPAS President Lynette Howell Taylor

articles.title: Goop Axes 20 Staff, Blames AI Just 18 Months After Last Cuts
articles.summary: Gwyneth Paltrow's Goop has let go of around 20 employees this week, citing 'profitability and AI workflow' as the reason. It is the second significant round of layoffs at Goop in under two years. The move raises fresh questions about whether AI is genuinely improving businesses or simply becoming a convenient excuse to cut headcount.
articles.content:
Gwyneth Paltrow's Goop has cut around 20 jobs this week, and the reason given is raising eyebrows across the industry. The company told departing staff the decision came down to 'profitability and AI workflow,' framing the cuts as a pivot toward AI-driven operations rather than a sign of financial trouble. That framing is doing a lot of heavy lifting.

This is not the first time Goop has been here. Just 18 months ago, the wellness and lifestyle brand went through a large-scale round of layoffs that already shook the company. Coming back to the well so soon suggests that whatever Goop tried to fix the first time around did not fully take.

The 'pivot to AI' explanation is becoming a familiar refrain in the business world right now, and it cuts two ways. On one hand, AI tools genuinely are replacing certain roles in content, customer service, and operations. On the other hand, companies have realised that slapping an AI rationale onto layoffs sounds more forward-thinking than admitting a brand is struggling to find its footing.

Goop built its reputation on Gwyneth Paltrow's personal brand and a willingness to sell a very specific kind of aspirational wellness lifestyle. But that space has gotten crowded and the economics are brutal. Whether AI really is doing the work of 20 people at Goop, or whether this is a cost-cutting exercise dressed up in tech language, the people who lost their jobs this week are feeling the consequences either way.
keyPoints: Goop cut around 20 employees this week, citing AI workflow and profitability
This is the second major round of layoffs at Goop in just 18 months
The company framed the cuts as a strategic pivot to AI-driven operations
The move fits a growing trend of companies using AI as a reason for reducing headcount
Goop's long-term business health remains a question mark after repeated cuts
